channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran Offers Plan to Focus on Strait of Hormuz and Delay Nuclear Talks
item.description: In its latest offer delivered on Sunday, Iran proposed opening the key waterway to shipping traffic and lifting the U.S. blockade, while postponing the thornier nuclear issue until later.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran last week.

item.title: Canada Says Its Gold Is Traceable and Clean. So We Traced It.
item.description: We ended up in a Colombian mine controlled by a drug cartel.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: This illegal mine near Caucasia, Colombia, sells gold into the Royal Canadian Mint’s supply chain.

item.title: Mali Terror Attack: Defense Minister Killed by Al Qaeda-Linked JNIM
item.description: The death of the defense minister, Gen. Sadio Camara, a central figure in the country’s military government, comes amid escalating violence in the region.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean and Max Kim
item.media:description: Gen. Sadio Camara of Mali in 2024. He was a central figure in the 2020 coup that ousted then-President Ibrahim Boubacar Keita.

item.title: Carney to Launch Sovereign Wealth Fund to Distance Canada’s Economy From U.S.
item.description: The sovereign wealth fund announced by Prime Minister Mark Carney will be far smaller than ones in other oil producers like Norway and the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada announcing a sovereign wealth fund on Monday in Ottawa.

item.title: Canada’s New Route to Citizenship Has Thousands of Americans Lining Up to Apply
item.description: Canada has opened a route to citizenship for people who can prove they have a Canada-born ancestor. Millions could qualify, and Americans are already lining up to apply.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: A citizenship ceremony for new Canadians last year in Montreal.

item.title: Hezbollah Vows to Keep Its Weapons as Lebanon Says Israeli Strikes Killed 14
item.description: Despite a cease-fire, Israel and Hezbollah have been trading attacks almost daily.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A funeral in Maaroub, southern Lebanon, on Sunday, which according to the Lebanese Health Ministry was one of the deadliest days in the country since a truce was declared.

item.title: Iran’s Foreign Minister Is in Russia for Talks With Putin on Middle East War
item.description: Abbas Araghchi, Iran’s foreign minister, met with President Vladimir V. Putin in Moscow. Russia has tried to avoid entanglement in the conflict while remaining a key player in the region.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Abbas Araghchi, Iran’s foreign minister, with President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia in St. Petersburg on Monday. Mr. Araghchi’s visit to Russia follows a weekend of talks in Pakistan and Oman.

item.title: King Charles Is Coming to the U.S. at a Very Delicate Time
item.description: Not since his mother, Queen Elizabeth II, traveled to Washington after the Suez Crisis has a visit by the British monarch come at such a fraught point in Anglo-American relations.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
item.media:description: King Charles III, center, and President Trump with a Coldstream Guardsman in September at Windsor Castle.

item.title: Fighting ‘Sacred’ War in Ukraine, North Korea and Russia Pledge to Deepen Ties
item.description: The two countries discussed long-term military cooperation, as they opened a memorial for North Korean troops killed in Russia’s war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Korean Central News Agency, via Reuters
item.media:description: A photograph provided by North Korean state media showed the country’s leader, Kim Jong-un, at the opening ceremony of a memorial in Pyongyang on Sunday. Defense Minister Andrei R. Belousov and Speaker Vyacheslav Volodin of Russia sit behind him.

item.title: Euphoria in Kenya After Sabastian Sawe’s Record-Breaking Win
item.description: Nowhere has the achievement at the race in London resonated more than in Kenya’s Rift Valley, which has yielded many champions.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and John Eligon
item.media:description: Sabastian Sawe ran the London Marathon in less than two hours on Sunday.

item.title: Homeowners Fight for Control of Their Community in China
item.description: Residents signed petitions, organized rallies and held strategy sessions over karaoke, debating how far to push the authorities in their dispute with a developer.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Wang and Qilai Shen
item.media:description: Homeowners discussing their negotiation strategy with management at a home at the Lafite Waterfront complex on the outskirts of Beijing.

item.title: Archbishop of Canterbury Prays With Pope Leo at the Vatican in Historic Meeting
item.description: The meeting highlighted deepening ties between two major Christian denominations, as well as longstanding differences on the ordination of women.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: A photo released by the Vatican on Monday showed Pope Leo meeting with the Most Rev. Sarah Mullally, the archbishop of Canterbury, at the Vatican.

item.title: Israel’s President, Putting Off Decision on Pardon for Netanyahu, Will Push for Plea Deal
item.description: President Isaac Herzog of Israel has decided not to issue a pardon to Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in his corruption case at this time, and instead will seek mediation, officials say.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel, left, with President Isaac Herzog, in Jerusalem last week.

item.title: U.S. Mint Buys Drug Cartel Gold and Sells It as ‘American’
item.description: As prices for the precious metal soar, the industry’s guardrails have broken down.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck, Simón Posada and Federico Rios
item.media:description:
This gold comes from a Colombian drug cartel mine. It should never end up at the U.S. Mint.
But it does.

item.title: How South Korea Uses A.I. to Check on Its Elderly
item.description: In the world’s fastest aging society, artificial intelligence is being used to make care calls to older adults who live alone and to fight dementia.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times

item.title: In Iran, Cheetah Sightings Offer Rare Spots of Hope Amid War
item.description: Conservationists say they have recorded several new adult and cub Asiatic cheetahs, a critically endangered subspecies found only in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A female Asiatic cheetah named Dalbar in the Pardisan Park in Tehran in 2017. The species is under constant threat of hunters, speeding cars and wild dogs.

item.title: Iraq Taps Businessman, Ali al-Zaidi, to Form New Government
item.description: After months of tensions and pressure from both the U.S. and Iran, Ali al-Zaidi, a businessman, was named as prime minister-designate.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: The ceremony in Baghdad on Monday at which Ali al-Zaidi was announced as prime minister-designate. The photograph was released by the president’s media office.

item.title: Mali Rebels Strike Major Blow Against Junta and Russia’s Africa Corps
item.description: Coordinated attacks signified a major escalation of insurgent violence in a region of West Africa where military leaders had seized power and warmed to Moscow in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh
item.media:description: Tuareg rebels in the Azawad Liberation Front coalition riding on the back of a pickups in Kidal, Mali, on Sunday.

item.title: The Art of the Nuclear Deal
item.description: President Trump ripped up a pact designed to prevent Iran from building a bomb. Getting a better agreement now could prove difficult.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom

item.title: United Airlines Says It Pitched American on Merger but Was Rebuffed
item.description: United’s chief acknowledged for the first time that he had broached the idea of a merger with American, which was unwilling to consider it.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Scott Kirby, chief of United Airlines, said a merger with American Airlines would have generated significant economic benefits for the United States.

item.title: Raghu Rai, Photographer Who Captured Modern India, Dies at 83
item.description: Mr. Rai documented major figures like the Dalai Lama and Indira Gandhi, as well as the victims of a poison gas leak in the city of Bhopal.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Suhasini Raj

item.title: China’s Economy Starts to Show Cracks From Iran War
item.description: China’s strategic reserves of oil and natural gas have insulated it somewhat, but its manufacturing-based economy is beginning to falter.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: Chinese-made cars waiting to be exported at a port in Suzhou, China, on Monday. China has sought to increase exports as demand has weakened at home.

item.title: China Will Require Meta to Unwind Acquisition of AI Start-Up Manus
item.description: The impact of the ruling was not immediately clear, but it could send a chilling signal to Chinese tech founders seeking to team up with foreign companies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta has described the two teams already as “deeply integrated.”

item.title: Iran War Shakes Global Economy, but the U.S. Has Mostly Been Spared
item.description: In just eight weeks, much of the global economy has been knocked sideways. America has mostly been spared from the tumult.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Linh Pham for The New York Times
item.media:description: A farmer in Vietnam preparing plastic containers of diesel he had just purchased.

item.title: U.S. Military Strikes Another Boat in Eastern Pacific, Killing 3
item.description: The United States has carried out dozens of boat strikes since September against people the Trump administration accuses of smuggling drugs.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:description: An image from a video provided by the U.S. military shows what it said was a strike on a boat in the eastern Pacific Ocean on Sunday.

item.title: 8 Memorable Moments From Past British Royal Visits to the U.S.
item.description: With King Charles III and Queen Camilla set to arrive for a state visit with President Trump, we look back at some major moments in previous royal trips to the United States.
item.dc:creator: Esther Bintliff
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
George VI, King of Great Britain
item.media:description: Queen Elizabeth and President Ronald Reagan in San Francisco, in 1983.

item.title: The Rising Chinese Automaker Not Named BYD
item.description: Geely is challenging the giant BYD by adapting quickly to swings in demand and energy prices, seizing on interest in electric vehicles prompted by the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: How a Trump Event Shooting Unfolded
item.description: The gunman had written a “manifesto,” the president said, before trying to enter a dinner in Washington for the White House press corps.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Former Israeli Premiers Join in Bid to Oust Netanyahu in Elections
item.description: Naftali Bennett, a right-wing politician, and Yair Lapid, a centrist, will merge parties for a vote later this year.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:description: Naftali Bennett, left, and Yair Lapid on Sunday in Herzliya, Israel, where they said they would unite in a party to be called Yachad, Hebrew for “together.”

item.title: What to Know About U.S.-Iran Peace Talks
item.description: An Iranian negotiator returned to Pakistan on Sunday, despite the United States abruptly calling off a trip there by its two top negotiators.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Posters highlighting talks in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Saturday.

item.title: Iran and U.S. Sink Into Awkward Limbo of ‘No War, No Peace’
item.description: Each side is betting it can last longer than the other, analysts say. But there are risks in a stalemate without a deal.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran depicting Iranian missiles attacking a U.S. Navy ship.

item.title: Islamabad Reopens After U.S.-Iran Talks Fail to Materialize
item.description: Officials had locked the city down, anticipating talks between U.S. and Iranian delegations. But they didn’t happen. “What did I close my business for?” one business owner asked.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A reopened road on Sunday near the venue that hosted the first round of talks between the United States and Iran in Islamabad, Pakistan.

item.title: Israel and Hezbollah Trade Strikes in Lebanon, as Iran Talks Remain on Hold
item.description: Cease-fires in Lebanon and Iran are on shaky ground, with military attacks flaring and direct talks between Washington and Tehran to end their war stalled.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A mass funeral in the southern Lebanese village of Maaroub on Sunday.

item.title: What Happened After The New York Times Found a Cartel Mine on a Colombian Military Base
item.description: Officers denied that an illegal, large-scale gold operation was underway within earshot of their posts. But we had seen it with our own eyes.
item.dc:creator: Federico Rios

item.title: How We Traced U.S. Government Gold to a Drug Cartel
item.description: Three reporters followed supply chains to reveal that the U.S. Mint buys gold that comes from foreign pawn shops and drug dealers, then claims it is from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The reporter Justin Scheck of The Times at La Mandinga, in Colombia. It’s a government-owned cattle ranch where illegal miners tear up the earth.

item.title: How Drug Cartel Gold Ends Up at the U.S. Mint
item.description: The U.S. Mint is legally required to sell only legal, domestic gold. Instead, it is the last link in a chain that launders foreign gold for an insatiable market. Our reporter Justin Scheck traced one such supply chain: from an illegal mine in Colombia all the way to the Mint’s facilities in West Point, N.Y.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck, Rebecca Suner, James Surdam, Lauren Pruitt and Federico Rios

item.title: In Japan, the Fight Is on Against Unruly Tourists
item.description: Huge crowds of visitors during cherry blossom season have put a picturesque Japanese town on edge. Some residents are trying to take charge.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández, Kiuko Notoya and Kentaro Takahashi
item.media:description: Tourists in April in Fujiyoshida, Japan.

item.title: What to Know About King Charles and Queen Camilla’s U.S. State Visit Hosted by Trump
item.description: The British royals are set to arrive on Monday for a visit hosted by President Trump, with a garden party, an address to Congress and a banquet on the schedule.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: King Charles and Queen Camilla in London this past week. When President Trump was asked whether Charles’s upcoming visit could mend relations with Britain, he said: “Absolutely. He’s fantastic. He’s a fantastic man. Absolutely the answer is yes.”

item.title: 40 Years After the Meltdown, War Layers Another Disaster on Chernobyl
item.description: Ideas have been floated for how the contaminated zone could bring economic benefits to Ukraine. But for the foreseeable future, it will be an army-controlled security belt.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer, Evelina Riabenko and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: 40 Years Ago, a Nuclear Catastrophe at Chernobyl
item.description: Photographs from the first days of the Chernobyl disaster and of the aftermath years later show the response, the evacuation and the long-term consequences of the world’s worst nuclear accident.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chernobyl nuclear power plant three days after the explosion in April 1986.

item.title: How Nieves González’s Painting of Lily Allen Made Her Famous
item.description: Nieves González, a 29-year-old painter, once worked in relative obscurity in Andalusia. Her picture of the British singer Lily Allen changed that.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times

item.title: Al Qaeda-Linked Militants Launch Major Attacks on Cities Across Mali
item.description: The armed group JNIM claimed to have seized two key cities and destroyed the defense minister’s residence in a coordinated offensive that experts said was a major escalation in yearslong hostilities.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad
item.media:description: A Malian soldier on guard outside Bamako, Mali, on Saturday.

item.title: Crushed at the Polls, Hungary’s Former Ruling Party Licks Its Wounds
item.description: Viktor Orban, who helped found the Fidesz party, said he would leave his Parliament seat but hoped to remain as head of his party.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins
item.media:description: Hungary’s departing prime minister, Viktor Orban, in Budapest this month.

item.title: Families of Iranian Children Killed in School Airstrike Pen Letter to Pope
item.description: Pope Leo said he has seen the letter from the parents of more than 100 children killed in the strike, which a preliminary inquiry found resulted from a mistake by the U.S. military.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial in Tehran for the victims of an airstrike on an elementary school in Minab, Iran, on the first day of U.S. and Israeli bombing of the country.

item.title: 2 C.I.A. Officers Killed in Mexico Crash Lacked Proper Authorization
item.description: The two Americans were killed on Sunday when their vehicle crashed while returning from an antidrug operation led by Mexico’s armed forces in the state of Chihuahua.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Attorney General'S Office of Chihuahua, via Reuters
item.media:description: A suspected methamphetamine processing lab in the northern Mexican state of Chihuahua.

item.title: Big-Game Hunter From California Is Killed by Elephant in Gabon
item.description: Ernie Dosio, 75, of Lodi, Calif., was hunting a species of antelope in the dense forests of Gabon when his hunting party stumbled on a small herd of female elephants with calves.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

item.title: Semyon Gluzman, 79, Dies; Doctor Dared to Criticize Soviet Psychiatry
item.description: He was imprisoned for calling attention to the practice of declaring dissidents mentally ill. He later led efforts to confront his field’s dark past.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: Trump Cancels Witkoff and Kushner’s Trip for Iran Talks, Saying, ‘We Have All the Cards.’
item.description: The last-minute scuttling of the trip to Pakistan was the latest sign of how far apart the two sides are on reaching a deal to end the Iran war.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Thursday. He decided on Saturday not to have two of his top negotiators travel to Pakistan.

item.title: Indigenous Speakers Are Booed During Commemorations of Australia’s War Dead
item.description: Dawn services for Anzac Day were disrupted in three cities. The hecklers seemed to be targeting a widespread Aboriginal custom meant to acknowledge the land’s original inhabitants.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:description: The Anzac Day service at the Shrine of Remembrance in Melbourne, Australia, on Saturday.

item.title: Lionel Rosenblatt Dies at 82; Led Daring Rescue of Vietnamese Refugees
item.description: In an unsanctioned mission, the Foreign Service officer helped evacuate about 200 South Vietnamese citizens from Saigon days before the city fell in 1975.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Lebanese Ask, ‘What Cease-Fire?’ as Israel and Hezbollah Trade Attacks in the South
item.description: The fragile truce in Lebanon was extended after talks in Washington this week but has come under threat as Israel and Hezbollah have traded attacks.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.media:description: A mass funeral in Ramadiyeh, Lebanon, on Friday, mourning those killed in recent weeks in the war with Israel.

item.title: High Lights
item.description: Our lives are governed by wondrous phenomena that we don’t often stop to consider.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: The Challenges Facing Canada as It Inches Toward Trade Talks
item.description: An international trade economist in Washington warns that negotiations will take place in an “incredibly difficult” environment.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.category: Peterson, Peter G, Institute for International Economics
item.media:description: Whether by chance or design, Prime Minister Mark Carney made a housing announcement this week in front of a Japanese excavator rather than a more commonly found American-made one.

item.title: The Elite ‘Doctors’ Who Care for Mount Everest
item.description: Glacial ice has sealed the gateway to Everest’s summit, keeping hundreds of climbers from reaching the top. “Icefall doctors” are on the case.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Binod Ghimire
item.media:description: A wall of ice has halted Mount Everest’s climbing season. Mountaineers tasked with maintaining the route, known as icefall doctors, are under pressure to clear a path.

item.title: Trump Seeks to Abolish Iran’s Nuclear Stockpile, a Problem He Helped Create
item.description: President Trump withdrew from the Obama-era nuclear accord in 2018, saying it was the worst deal ever. But Iran responded with an enrichment spree that haunts the negotiations to this day.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: In February in preparation for a possible war with the United States, Iran moved missile launchers into positions within striking distance of Israeli and American military forces.

item.title: Under Trump, Green Card Seekers Face New Scrutiny for Views on Israel
item.description: In guidance to immigration officers, the administration describes participating in pro-Palestinian protests and criticizing Israel as “overwhelmingly negative” factors.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:description: Supporters of Palestine demonstrating in New York last year. Under new guidance issued by the Trump administration, immigrants could be denied a green card for participating in pro-Palestinian protests.

item.title: Europe Prepares for a Longer War in Ukraine, With No Strategy to End It
item.description: With American dealmakers wrapped up with Iran, neither Russia nor Ukraine has a clear path to victory — or toward a negotiated peace.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers training this month in the Chernobyl exclusion zone.

item.title: Raqqa Showcases Syria’s Tumultuous Past
item.description: The city on the Euphrates, once a cradle of civilization, suffered under a succession of recent rulers. Our visit found it hoping for change.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall, Hussam Hammoud and Nanna Heitmann
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:description: Dancing in Naim Square, where ISIS once held public executions, in January after Syrian government forces captured Raqqa.

item.title: AIDS Creeps Back in Parts of Zambia, a Year After U.S. Cuts to H.I.V. Assistance
item.description: A once-robust H.I.V. treatment and prevention system, credited with saving hundreds of thousands of lives, has begun to crumble.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maureen Dhaka, left, with her baby in the neonatal unit at Arthur Davison Children’s Hospital in Ndola, Zambia.

item.title: On Iran’s Border, Cooking Oil Trade Is a Snapshot of the Country’s Struggles
item.description: The war has exacerbated Iran’s economic crisis, forcing many to cross the border into Turkey to buy the most basic goods.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kiana Hayeri for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians carrying cooking oil on Wednesday at the Kapikoy border crossing between Turkey and Iran.

item.title: Japan’s New Care Workers: Bodybuilders, Wrestlers and M.M.A. Fighters
item.description: Young athletes are being recruited to plug a staff shortage in Japan’s nursing homes. The results are reshaping elder care.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee, Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
